title: Electron Doping of $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ Thin Films: Candidate Metal Dopants and Their Potential Impact on Superconductivity
abstract: The bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ has emerged as a promising platform for exploring and understanding high-temperature superconductivities. While most prior doping studies   have focused on hole doping via strontium (Sr) substitution or by tuning oxygen content, electron doping remains largely unexplored. In this work,we systematically investigate electron doping in $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ thin films through   tetravalent element substitution, employing first-principles density functional theory calculations.   Our results reveal that, unlike in cuprates, $\mathrm{cerium}$ (Ce) doping is difficult to effectively introduce electron carriers into the low-energy bands. In contrast, zirconium (Zr), hafnium (Hf), and thorium (Th) can act as efficient electron dopants. These element substitutions can significantly increase the interlayer hopping $t_{\perp}$ between $d_{z^2}$ orbitals, which may lead to enhanced superexchange coupling $J_{\perp}$ , and thereby potentially elevated superconducting $T_c$. We evaluate the interaction parameters using constrained random phase approximation. Our results identify candidate dopants for achieving electron-doped $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$, offering a route to clarify the ongoing debate on pairing mechanisms in this system.
summary: This theoretical study investigates electron doping in $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ using DFT calculations to understand its superconducting potential. The authors find that $\mathrm{Zr}$, $\mathrm{Hf}$, and $\mathrm{Th}$ are effective electron dopants, which enhances interlayer coupling ($t_{\perp}$) and suggests a pathway to higher $T_c$. The findings help clarify the complex pairing mechanisms in this class of nickelate superconductors.
main_problem: The study investigates electron doping in the Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ to understand its potential impact on high-temperature superconductivity, contrasting it with existing hole-doping studies.
main_result: The authors identify $\mathrm{Zr}$, $\mathrm{Hf}$, and $\mathrm{Th}$ as efficient electron dopants, which can increase the interlayer hopping $t_{\perp}$ and potentially enhance the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$).
method: The research employs first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations, supplemented by the constrained random-phase approximation (cRPA) to estimate effective screened interactions.
model_or_system: The physical system is the bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ thin film, modeled with substitutions $\mathrm{La_{3-x}R_xNi_2O_7}$ where R is the dopant element. The analysis focuses on the electronic structure and interaction parameters governing superconductivity.
key_observables: Interlayer hopping integral ($t_{\perp}$), inter-layer superexchange coupling ($J_{\perp}$), electron counts in $e_g$ orbitals ($\mathrm{d}_{z^2}$ and $\mathrm{d}_{x^2-y^2}$), and Hubbard interaction parameters ($ ar{U}$, $J_H/U$).
important_parameters: The key parameters include the dopant identity ($\mathrm{Zr}, \mathrm{Hf}, \mathrm{Th}$ vs. $\mathrm{Ce}$), the ratio $t_{\perp}/t_{1x}$, and the strength of Hund's coupling ($J_H/U$).
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the superconducting properties are governed by the electronic structure changes induced by doping, particularly the modification of hopping integrals and correlation parameters.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate energy bands for various doped films, structural changes like bond length variations, and the fitting of band structures using a double-layer Hubbard model. Tables summarize calculated interaction parameters.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the effect of different dopants (electron vs. hole) on the electronic structure of $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ using DFT. It then uses these results to calculate key superconducting parameters like $t_{\perp}$ and $J_{\perp}$, proposing a unified mechanism for superconductivity based on the doping type.
why_interesting: The work provides detailed theoretical insights into tuning superconductivity in nickelates by controlling electron doping, which is crucial for understanding unconventional pairing mechanisms in strongly correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Energy bands of 1UC La2−xRxNi2O7 thin films. (a) La2CeNi2O7, (b) La2ThNi2O7, (c) La2ZrNi2O7, (d) La2HfNi2O7. The dz2 and dx2−y2 orbitals weights in low- energy bands are denoted by red (dark) and blue (light) dots respectively. Low-energy bands of pristine La3Ni2O7 film on substrate are shown by gray lines for reference. Here substrate with a = 3.905 ˚A is assumed. For La2CeNi2O7 DFT+U is also applied to the 4f−orbitals of Ce. See also Appendix.

Figure 2. (a) Structure of the 1UC La3Ni2O7 thin film, where h represents the distance between the apical oxygen atoms at the ends of the 1UC. The dashed box is enlarged to show the sites for electron doping, the pristine La3Ni2O7 structure, and hole doping. Here, different chemical bonds are labeled: d0–d4 are Ni–O bonds, and d5 is the R–O bond. (b) Bond length indicated in (a) for the thin-film La3Ni2O7 and La2RNi2O7 (R = La, Ce, Zr, Hf, Th, and Sr).(c)Bond lengths as functions of substrate in-plane lattices constant a.

Fig. 2c shows the variation of vertical Ni-O bond lengths d0 to d3 of pristine La3Ni2O7 on different sub- strates (LaAlO3, 3.77 ˚A) and tensile (NdGaO3, 3.855 ˚A; SrTiO3, 3.905 ˚A) [14, 70]. It can be seen that all verti- cal Ni-O bonds, except for d2 that remains nearly con- stant, generally decrease with increasing in-plane lattice constant. Doped compounds exhibit similar trend upon lattice constant variation (result not shown).

Figure 3. Density of states of the thin-film La2ANi2O7. (a–b) The density of states for Ni and O, for t2g and eg orbitals, respectively. (c-d) The density of states of the in-plane Ni- dz2 orbital and its associated O-px and -py orbitals, as well as the out-of-plane Ni dz2 orbital and its associated O-pz orbital.

Figure 4. Average charge transfer between O, Ni, La, and R (La, Zr, Hf, Th, Sr) elements. Here, O gains charge, while the other atoms lose charge. Dotted line denotes result of La3Ni2O7 for reference.
